summary: Fowler explains why he avoids creating video content despite its growing popularity and commercial viability. While acknowledging that video suits his public speaking abilities and that audiences clearly value it, he finds the medium personally unappealing for both learning and creating. His core objection is that writing serves as a thinking tool—he figures things out through the act of writing, whereas video feels like it requires pre-formed ideas. The essay format allows for exploration and discovery that video production doesn't seem to support.
key_points: Reading is faster than listening and allows for easy navigation—skipping ahead or jumping back
Audio has a niche advantage over video: it can be consumed while walking or driving
Many people publish video talks but miss the opportunity to provide audio-only versions
Writing is a tool for thinking, not just explaining already-formed ideas
The word 'essay' comes from French 'essayer' meaning 'to try'—writing is trying out ideas
money_quotes: I can read much faster than I can listen.
More than once I've been frustrated by people who publish videos of talks, but miss the opportunity to do an audio-only version.
When I'm writing, I'm not usually explaining things I've already figured out, I'm doing the figuring out as I write.
The act of writing something down has often helped me understand a topic, indeed I think that's a large part of why I write as much as I do.
I enjoy trying to understand things, and writing for others is a vital tool to help me do that.
key_insight: Writing is not merely a way to communicate ideas you've already formed, but a tool for discovering and refining those ideas in the first place.
